title: Dusty May Commits To Michigan, Won't Leave For North Carolina Job
summary: Michigan head coach Dusty May has told university officials he is not seeking other coaching positions, according to ESPN sources. The move effectively ends any possibility of May landing at North Carolina, which had identified him as an early candidate. May's pursuit was never serious given Michigan's run to Monday's national championship game against UConn. The Wolverines finished 36-3, claimed the Big Ten regular-season title and recorded 29 double-digit victories. "I think it's well-documented how happy I am at Michigan," May said Friday. "Obviously, my private life, my personal life, my family, their happiness is very important." May signed a contract extension last year that runs through 2030. Departing for another program this spring would trigger a $7 million buyout. North Carolina dismissed Hubert Davis last month following five seasons. The Tar Heels surrendered a 19-point lead to VCU in the first round, marking back-to-back first-round exits. Arizona's Tommy Lloyd, considered the primary target for the Chapel Hill post, agreed to a fresh five-year contract to remain in Tucson on Friday. With both Lloyd and May unavailable, Chicago Bulls head coach Billy Donovan is expected to emerge as a leading candidate for the Carolina vacancy.

title: Increased Belief Giannis Antetokounmpo Will Be Traded This Summer
summary: Expectations are increasing that the Milwaukee Bucks will trade Giannis Antetokounmpo this summer, according to Marc Stein of The Stein Line. The NBA is currently investigating the Bucks over their handling of the player participation policy regarding Antetokounmpo. The Bucks told investigators that Antetokounmpo declined participating in 3-on-3 scrimmages as part of his return protocol. Antetokounmpo has told that league that he is healthy enough to return from a hyperextended left knee and bone bruise suffered on March 15. There was already a strong belief that the Bucks would trade Antetokounmpo prior to their latest disagreement. Bucks governor Wes Edens also told ESPN that Antetokounmpo would either sign an extension or get traded this offseason, with Antetokounmpo describing the ultimatum as " like a slap in my face. " Antetokounmpo described his strained relationship with the Bucks' front office, saying that he doesn't feel "comfortable talking to nobody."

title: UCLA Beats South Carolina 79-51 For First Women's NCAA Title
summary: UCLA won its first NCAA women's basketball championship Sunday, routing South Carolina 79-51 in the national title game in Phoenix. The 28-point margin ranks as the third largest in Division I women's championship history. The Bruins never trailed, building a double-digit lead after the first quarter and extending their winning streak to 31 games. Lauren Betts was named the Final Four's Most Outstanding Player. "We got whooped. Not beat. We got whooped," South Carolina's Raven Johnson said. Five UCLA players reached double figures. Gabriela Jaquez led all scorers with 21 points, 10 rebounds and five assists. Betts added 14 points and 11 rebounds. South Carolina's three primary contributors, Joyce Edwards, Ta'Niya Latson and Johnson, combined for just 15 points on 5-of-22 shooting. The Gamecocks finished at 29% from the field, their worst shooting performance in tournament history. UCLA opened the second half on a 12-3 run to put the game beyond reach. The victory ended a 48-year wait since the program's 1978 AIAW title, with that era's standout Ann Meyers Drysdale in attendance Sunday. "My dream was to be at UCLA," Jaquez said. "We were determined, the core group, to do something UCLA hadn't done before in the NCAA era. We always believed. Job is finished." Coach Cori Close, in her 15th season leading the program, became the longest-tenured single-school head coach to claim a first NCAA championship. "It's immeasurably more than I could ask or imagine," Close said. "It's beyond my wildest dreams."

title: Giannis Antetokounmpo Opens Up On Bucks Relationship, Calls Ultimatum 'Slap In Face'
summary: Giannis Antetokounmpo spoke candidly with the Milwaukee Journal Sentinel about his interpretation of his relationship with the Milwaukee Bucks franchise as they continue down a more acrimonious path. As Antetokounmpo becomes eligible for a pivotal five-year, $275 million contract extension in October, he and the Bucks are heading towards an inflection point. Bucks governor Wes Edens told ESPN that Antetokounmpo would either commit to signing the extension or would be traded this offseason. "For me, it's like a slap in my face," said Antetokounmpo. During the 2025-26 season, Antetokounmpo has had just one Zoom call with Edens and Jimmy Haslam, while also having an additional conversation around a game with Haslam. This lack of communication led Antetokounmpo to find out about the Bucks' position on his future through the media. "It says a lot ..." Antetokounmpo said. "Especially [as] somebody that has been this loyal, and I've showed appreciation, to the team, to the community, to the fans, to everybody that I've worked with, you know?" Antetokounmpo said. "And that's almost, like, a slap in my face." Antetokounmpo said that the Bucks have asked him to address trade rumors, which he did first at the start of the season and again in February. "I'm here, I'm committed to the team, and I'm committed to Milwaukee because I love this city," Antetokounmpo repeated in this interview. His wish is this: "To the organization, the conversations that I have between me, myself, and my bosses − it's private. It should not be in the media. If they're willing, they want to share it to the media, they can go ahead and do that." Speculation on a potential trade intensified beginning at last year's lottery, but no actual trade negotiations became serious. "I'm here, I committed, I'm wearing the jersey, and it is disrespectful to my teammates, to my coaching staff, to the people that I go and sweat with, and go to war with, to say, 'I don't want to be here' − and I will never do that," Antetokounmpo said again this week. "Now, what goes on in private rooms? It's not my job, it's not my responsibility, and it should not − in no shape or form − be shared in public. I believe in etiquette. I believe in human etiquette, I believe in basketball etiquette. So with my owners, I have addressed what I feel and what I believe the situation is. "Same with my GM, same with my coaches. "And for me, that [Wes Edens] comment was a slap [in] the face. But, it's my boss. So I just gotta keep on moving forward." Antetokounmpo also suggested his contract is his best leverage to impact the situation. Previous major trades by the Bucks came in 2020 ahead of Antetokounmpo's first supermax extension when they acquired Jrue Holiday, and again in 2023 when they traded for Damian Lillard. "Could they downplay my contract?" he said. "1000%. 'Yeah, he's a Buck for life. Yeah, whatever.' "I totally agree with you. That's my one, of my leverage, let's say. "But again, I have declared that [I'm a Buck] many times, and I've said the most-important thing, which I believe in, and I would say again." Antetokounmpo's communication with the Bucks' front office has also become more strained. "Nobody," he said. "I don't feel comfortable talking to nobody." Antetokounmpo continues to talk to general manager Horst, but less than before. "I tried this whole year to stay to myself, and also this past summer," Antetokounmpo said. "I was more closed off to myself. I don't know if that's because I don't feel comfortable opening up enough. Or if it's just, I just don't want to open up at this point. "It's definitely not like before."

Big Ten Player of the Year Yaxel Lendeborg was diagnosed with a left MCL sprain and an ankle injury following the game. Lendeborg suffered the injury while driving to the hoop in the first half but played nine minutes in the second half. Michigan lead by as much as 30 points in the second half. The Wolverines also became the first team in tournament history to score 90-plus points in five straight games. Michigan opened as -7.5 point favorites versus UConn.

title: Lauren Betts, UCLA Defense Beat Texas In Low-Scoring Final Four Semifinal
summary: UCLA ended Texas's tournament run Friday in a grinding 51-44 semifinal victory, securing the program's first-ever NCAA championship game berth. The Bruins will face South Carolina on Sunday. Lauren Betts delivered 16 points and 11 rebounds to pace UCLA. The win avenged the Bruins' only defeat of the season, a 76-65 loss to Texas on Nov. 26, capping a 30-game winning streak since that setback. "All you can ask for is an opportunity to play your best basketball for a national championship," UCLA coach Cori Close said. Texas star Madison Booker finished with a season-low six points on 3-of-23 shooting. Her 17 consecutive missed attempts set a Final Four record for the longest individual scoring drought. "We couldn't make a shot tonight, and that's been my fear the last three days," Texas coach Vic Schaefer said. "I think we feel like in our locker room, we let one get away. I think this one will haunt me as the coach for probably 'til the day I die." The game ranked among the lowest-scoring in Final Four history, with both teams combining for fewer than 100 points, only the third time that has occurred. UCLA committed 23 turnovers, the most in a Final Four since 2008. Texas shot 30.8% from the field. A 7-0 fourth-quarter surge, sparked by a Kiki Rice three-pointer and a Gabriela Jaquez layup, pushed UCLA's lead to double digits. Texas trimmed the gap to 47-44 with 55 seconds left before Betts rejected a Booker layup attempt with 20 seconds remaining, effectively closing the door. "Last year we took that loss really hard," Betts said. "I'm just really proud of the growth and the way that we've held such a high standard consistently this year."

title: Steph Curry Says Managing Knee Injury Is 'New Normal'
summary: Golden State Warriors guard Steph Curry discussed his frustration in dealing with an "unpredictable" knee injury ahead of his expected return on Sunday against the Houston Rockets. Curry has missed 27 games with "runner's knee." "I thought I was going to be out a week," Curry said. "Ten days max. [But] every time I got on the court or tried to push it in that first month, there was always a reaction. You just knew it wasn't healing as fast as you thought." Curry, who completed a 5-on-5 scrimmage, was cleared by the medical staff to play on Sunday. The 38-year-old stated that managing the knee would be a "new normal" as he continues his career. "Yes and no," Curry said. "There's nothing structurally wrong with my knee. So it's not like I'm compromised out there. It is a new normal, though, if that makes sense." Curry added that dealing with the knee was different from previous injuries because there was no set timetable. "Hopefully win two play-in games," Curry said about his expectations for the rest of the season. "Then we can have another conversation."
